summary:
The development of the microscope (c. 1590–1670s AD) opened an entirely new world to human perception — the realm of the microscopically small — and created the foundations of microbiology, cell theory, and the understanding of disease that would ultimately transform medicine. The compound microscope was developed in the Netherlands around 1590, attributed variously to spectacle-makers Hans and Zacharias Janssen in Middelburg. The instrument was significantly improved by Galileo around 1609, but its revolutionary scientific potential was fully realized by Antonie van Leeuwenhoek, a Dutch draper and amateur scientist who ground lenses of unprecedented quality in the 1670s, achieving magnifications of up to 270x — far beyond any contemporary instrument.

Leeuwenhoek's observations through his microscopes constituted one of the great observational achievements in scientific history. He was the first person to observe and describe bacteria (which he called 'animalcules'), protozoa, sperm cells, blood cells, and muscle fibers — an entirely new level of biological organization previously invisible to humanity. His reports to the Royal Society of London, written in Dutch and translated, sparked widespread astonishment and skepticism: the existence of invisible living creatures was philosophically startling. Robert Hooke's contemporaneous Micrographia (1665) — illustrated with stunning engravings of cork cells, fleas, and snowflakes — made microscopy accessible to educated Europe and coined the word 'cell' for the basic unit of life. These observations laid the groundwork for cell theory, established formally in 1839, and ultimately for germ theory and the bacteriological revolution of the 19th century.
